titleEn: Spring Innovation Fund debuts as first venture philanthropy firm dedicated to animal welfare tech
titleZh: Spring创新基金：首家专注动物福利技术的风险慈善机构
summaryZh: Spring创新基金作为首家专注于动物福利技术的风险慈善工作室，利用慈善资本孵化和投资初创企业，旨在通过低成本人道屠宰、疾病早期检测、AI与机器人等技术，在商业规模上改善养殖动物福利，同时实现商业回报。该基金采用“常青”模式，收益回流再投资，已识别100多个潜在空白领域，初期聚焦虾类人道屠宰、肉鸡福利等方向，为智慧畜牧行业提供了技术商业化与伦理结合的新路径。
featuredReason: 该基金聚焦低成本人道屠宰、AI疾病检测等技术与商业化的结合，为智慧畜牧行业提供了将伦理与效率融合的创新模式，值得关注技术落地与投资趋势的从业者阅读。
contentFull: Are animal welfare and economics fundamentally at odds? Not necessarily, say the founders of Spring Innovation Fund, a new venture studio betting that welfare tech can improve living conditions for billions of farmed animals while also delivering commercial returns. The brainchild of Mission Barns founder Eitan Fischer, former Blue Horizons principal Nate Crosser, and New Crop Capital founder Milo Runkle, Spring Innovation Fund is a first-of-its-kind venture studio using philanthropic capital to create and support startups that can improve animal welfare at commercial scale. A 501(c)(3) nonprofit, Spring Innovation Fund brings together tech, founders, and capital, incubating new companies and investing in existing ones. The studio runs a paid three-month entrepreneur-in-residence program, after which selected founders may launch companies with roughly $500,000 in initial funding. Spring may also provide follow-on funding and help companies access additional sources of capital, including venture capital, loans, grants, or other non-dilutive funding. Spring’s capital comes from private foundations and philanthropic donors who do not take equity stakes in the startups they help launch. However, Spring itself may take stakes in the for-profit companies it helps create, says Fischer. If those companies generate returns, the proceeds will flow back into the nonprofit so they can be recycled into future ventures, creating an “evergreen” model. Connecting tech, founders, and capital Spring has already secured some commitments and expects to deploy an eight-figure amount over the first year or two, Fischer told AgFunderNews. “Our goal is to connect great technology ideas or developments with the right founders and the right capital. We’re focused on where we believe an intervention can significantly improve animal welfare but also has a commercially plausible path to market.” To date, the studio has identified 100+ potential white spaces, drawing from academic research, animal welfare organizations and producers, with early areas of interest including low-cost humane stunning for crustaceans, broiler welfare, early disease detection, enrichment technologies, AI, and robotics. The initial plan is to home in on three to four areas where it can bridge the gap between promising science and commercial adoption. Today’s shrimp stunning systems are priced for the largest processors, leaving the vast majority of producers with no humane option, claims Spring Innovation Fund, which is helping to develop a stunning device built for everyday producers at a fraction of the cost of incumbent systems designed to make humane slaughter the default across the industry. Image credit: istock/chuangxin zhou Animal welfare tech and alt protein complement each other While Spring’s three founders have all spent years in the alt protein arena, Fischer says their move into animal ag should not be seen as an admission of defeat but a complementary strategy to ease animal suffering given the lengthy timelines associated with scaling alt protein. As for incentive structures around animal welfare, regulatory compliance is a bigger driver in Europe than the US or Asia, acknowledged Fischer. However, regulations in some European markets such as bans on male chick culling might provide the initial stimulus to develop tech such as in ovo sexing that might then go on to gain traction in markets outside of Europe, he noted. Similarly, it may be that first generation tech in this space could be supported through a small number of consumers willing to pay a premium for better welfare, especially if firms can tie that to other benefits. Second-generation systems deployed at scale may also offer producers a clearer economic payoff. With in-ovo sexing, for example, every day spent not incubating eggs containing male chicks can potentially save money in energy, labor, and hatchery capacity. AI, robotics, biotech… Technology can improve welfare and make business sense on farms in dozens of ways, said Fischer, highlighting AI-based monitoring tech for early disease detection and intervention, and enrichment for farmed fish to reduce stress levels, which has a direct impact on core business metrics such as disease and mortality rates. “Welfare tech can serve many functions such as preventing or treating painful diseases, obviating harmful practices, providing environmental enrichment, easing transportation stress and cost, mitigating wasteful mortality, monitoring individual health, improving diets, and more.” He added: “We’re finding some really exciting white spaces around enrichment: getting animals who are bored or not moving much, maybe are aggressive toward each other, giving them some opportunities to engage in more natural behaviors. There are a lot of studies showing that this reduces disease burden, enables animals to be potentially more productive, but also increases their welfare.” The best welfare innovations do not ask producers to choose between doing right by animals and running a viable business, he stressed. “They make the better choice the economically sensible one.” Advances in AI, robotics, and biotechnology are increasingly being deployed in animal ag, he noted, with on-farm computer-vision monitoring and wearables becoming affordable enough to deploy at commercial scale, turning animal health and welfare into something producers can measure and act on continuously. Innovations in biotechnology have also opened the door to better genetics, more practical vaccines, and safer biologics. Exit strategies While some of the technologies cited above are more capex-intensive than others, animal welfare tech has a sizable addressable market and strategic acquirers such as Zoetis, Merck Animal Health, and Elanco, he added. “For a lot of these opportunities, the bottleneck is not the science but crossing the valley of death to commercialization, so we’re looking at those early bets where a purely commercial VC may not necessarily want to take on that risk.” He added: “But some of these companies we certainly expect would be able to go on to raise a more traditional venture capital round, and potentially deliver the kind of exits that make sense for venture capital.” Share this article Sign up for our weekly newsletter. Biosphere picks up NovoNutrients assets in gas fermentation shakeout Alternative protein Biomanufacturing & biologicals Stenon bags $20.5m to combat fertilizer price hikes with better nitrogen management Agtech Artificial intelligence
translationZh:
动物福利与经济从根本上是对立的吗？Spring创新基金的创始人认为未必如此。这家新成立的风险工作室押注福利技术既能改善数十亿养殖动物的生活条件，又能带来商业回报。

Spring创新基金由Mission Barns创始人Eitan Fischer、前Blue Horizons负责人Nate Crosser以及New Crop Capital创始人Milo Runkle共同创立，是首个利用慈善资本创建和支持初创企业、以商业规模改善动物福利的风险工作室。作为一家501(c)(3)非营利组织，Spring创新基金将技术、创始人和资本汇聚在一起，孵化新公司并投资现有企业。该工作室运营一个为期三个月的付费驻场企业家项目，入选的创始人可能获得约50万美元的初始资金创办公司。Spring还可能提供后续资金，并帮助企业获取其他资本来源，包括风险投资、贷款、赠款或其他非稀释性资金。

Spring的资金来自私人基金会和慈善捐赠者，他们不持有所帮助启动的初创企业的股权。但Fischer表示，Spring本身可能持有其帮助创建的营利性公司的股份。如果这些公司产生回报，收益将回流到非营利组织，以便循环用于未来的项目，形成一种“常青”模式。

**连接技术、创始人和资本**

Fischer告诉AgFunderNews，Spring已获得一些承诺，预计在第一两年内部署八位数的资金。“我们的目标是将伟大的技术想法或发展与合适的创始人和资本连接起来。我们专注于那些我们认为能够显著改善动物福利、同时具备商业可行市场路径的干预措施。”

迄今为止，该工作室已从学术研究、动物福利组织和生产商中识别出100多个潜在空白领域，早期关注领域包括低成本人道甲壳类动物电击、肉鸡福利、早期疾病检测、富集技术、人工智能和机器人技术。初步计划聚焦三到四个领域，以弥合有前景的科学与商业应用之间的差距。

Spring创新基金表示，目前的虾类电击系统价格仅适合大型加工商，绝大多数生产商没有人道选择。该基金正在帮助开发一款面向日常生产商的低成本电击设备，其价格仅为现有系统的零头，旨在让人道屠宰成为行业默认标准。

**动物福利技术与替代蛋白相辅相成**

尽管Spring的三位创始人都在替代蛋白领域深耕多年，但Fischer表示，他们进入动物农业领域不应被视为承认失败，而是一种补充策略，以减轻动物痛苦。
author: Elaine Watson
